findings.title: _normalize_edges: O(N²) candidates scan with doubled work on raw_graph
findings.description: For every name in sorted_names (N distinct table names), the code builds a candidate list by scanning all N names with a linear comprehension. This is O(N²) in the number of distinct table names. At 500 tables the inner scan runs ~250,000 string comparisons per parse, and critically this function is called TWICE — once for all_edges and once for all_raw_edges (line 143). Because all_raw_edges always contains at least as many edges as all_edges (it is the pre-resolution superset), the table-name set on the second call may be equal or larger. This doubles the O(N²) cost on every refresh. For 500 tables: ~500,000 endswith string comparisons per refresh.
findings.evidence: engine.py:73-77: `candidates = [longer for longer in sorted_names if longer != name and longer.endswith('.' + name)]`. Called at line 142 for all_edges then line 143 for all_raw_edges unconditionally.
findings.fix: Build a suffix-indexed lookup structure once. Group names by their dot-suffix components (e.g., store a dict mapping each possible suffix token to a list of full names that end in `.{token}`). A two-pass approach — build the index in O(N * depth) where depth <= 3 for catalog.schema.table — then look up in O(1) per name. Alternatively, since the name depth is at most 3 parts, simply bucket by the last component and scan only the same-suffix bucket; the inner scan shrinks from O(N) to O(N / unique_last_parts). Sharing the computed short_to_long map between both _normalize_edges calls (since the set of table names is the same union) would also eliminate the full second O(N²) pass.
findings.impact: At 500 tables and two calls: ~500,000 string comparisons at parse time. Grows quadratically — 1,000 tables → ~2,000,000 comparisons.

findings.title: delete_source and refresh_source: O(edges) linear scan on global state graph for source file membership
findings.description: Both delete_source and refresh_source scan every edge in state.lineage_graph (and state.raw_graph) to find edges belonging to the source being deleted/refreshed. With multiple sources registered, the global graph accumulates edges from all sources, so this is O(total_edges_across_all_sources) per refresh of any one source. With 5 sources of 500 files each (a realistic deployment), a single refresh or delete touches ~5x more edges than necessary. The raw_graph is scanned separately, doubling the work.
findings.evidence: routes.py:127-131: `edges_to_remove = [(u, v) for u, v, d in state.lineage_graph.edges(data=True) if d.get('data') and d['data'].source_file in old_files]` — full graph scan. Repeated at lines 134-138 for raw_graph. Same pattern in refresh at lines 210-223.
findings.fix: Index edges by source_id (or by source_file set) at build time. When building the new_graph from a source, tag each edge's data with the source_id (already available via source_file which encodes source_ref). Maintain a per-source edge index in state so removal is O(edges_for_this_source) not O(total_edges). Alternatively, since edges carry source_file which encodes source_ref, build a state.edge_index: dict[source_id, set[tuple[str,str]]] updated on each refresh.
findings.impact: With 5+ sources each holding thousands of edges, each refresh/delete is 5x slower than needed and scales with total graph size, not per-source size.

findings.title: refresh_source: second full edge scan to build file_stats, avoidably separate from graph build
findings.description: After building new_graph via build_graph_with_warnings (which already iterates all edges internally), refresh_source does a second full scan of new_graph.edges(data=True) to accumulate per-file stats. This is O(edges_in_new_graph). The stat collection could be folded into engine.py's build loop, or the raw edge list (all_edges) could be passed back. This is a doubled pass over an already-traversed structure.
findings.evidence: routes.py:233-240: `for _, _, d in new_graph.edges(data=True): if d.get('data') and d['data'].source_file: ...` — executes after build_graph_with_warnings already iterated all edges.
findings.fix: Return per-file stats from build_graph_with_warnings as a third return value, computed during the existing edge-to-graph insertion loop at engine.py:154-155. Eliminates the second O(E) scan entirely. Alternatively pass the all_edges list back so routes.py can iterate it without touching the graph again.
findings.impact: Doubles edge-scanning work on every refresh. At 50,000 edges this is measurable but not severe — P2 because the absolute cost is low relative to parsing. Would become P1 if graph size grows significantly.

findings.title: _trace_raw_paths DFS: visited set does not persist across sibling branches, enabling redundant re-exploration
findings.description: The DFS backtracks the visited set (`visited.discard(pred)` at line 403), which is correct for path enumeration but means the same node can be re-explored via different parent branches. With max_paths=50 and max_depth=10, this is bounded in practice. However, for wide-fan-in graphs (a column used in many CTEs), the DFS could visit the same deep subtree many times before hitting the path cap. Unlike a memoized DFS, this recomputes get_preds() for the same node every time it is reached from a different parent.
findings.evidence: routes.py:401-403: `visited.add(pred); dfs(pred, steps_so_far + [step], visited, depth + 1); visited.discard(pred)` — classic backtracking, no result caching.
findings.fix: Cache get_preds() results in a local dict keyed by node (predecessors are static for the duration of the call). The visited.discard pattern is correct for path correctness but the edge lookup is redundant. `preds_cache: dict[str, list] = {}` at the closure level, then `preds = preds_cache.setdefault(node, get_preds(node, set()))` — note visited must be excluded dynamically so the cache stores the full predecessor list and the visited filter applies at call time.
findings.impact: Bounded by max_paths=50 / max_depth=10, so not a runaway scenario. Mainly felt on dense graphs where many paths share a common deep ancestor.

findings.title: WHERE filter edges: unbounded per-statement growth multiplied across all statements
findings.description: Every column reference in a WHERE predicate emits a dedicated LineageEdge to `target.__filter__`. A complex HAVING/WHERE clause with 20 column references emits 20 filter edges per statement. In a file with 200 SELECT statements (realistic for a large Databricks notebook), this is up to 4,000 extra filter edges per file, all flowing to the same synthetic `__filter__` target node. These edges inflate both lineage_graph and raw_graph and are scanned on every refresh, delete, and file-stats pass. The `seen_filter_src` set per statement deduplicates within a statement but not across statements.
findings.evidence: sql.py:396-407: one LineageEdge appended per unique col_ref per WHERE clause, with no cross-statement dedup or per-file cap.
findings.fix: Consider a per-file or per-table cap on filter edges (e.g., max 10 per target table). Alternatively, collapse multiple filter edges to the same target.__filter__ into a single edge with a list of sources in the expression field. At minimum, document that `__filter__` nodes should be excluded from graph size metrics to avoid misleading growth signals.
findings.impact: Linear growth with WHERE clause complexity times statement count. At 200 statements × 20 filter cols = 4,000 extra edges per file × 100 files = 400,000 extra edges added to global graph, inflating all O(edges) scans.

findings.title: list_columns: O(nodes) full graph scan + O(preds) per node, no index
findings.description: list_columns iterates all nodes in state.lineage_graph to find those belonging to a specific table. At 10,000 nodes (500 tables × 20 columns each), this is a 10,000-node scan per API call. Each matching node then calls `graph.predecessors(node)` and iterates preds to build source_tables. This is O(nodes + edges_for_table), called on every column-list request with no caching.
findings.evidence: routes.py:301-315: `for node in state.lineage_graph.nodes(): if '.' in node: t, col = node.rsplit('.', 1); if t == table: ...` — full linear scan every call.
findings.fix: Maintain an in-memory index `state.table_index: dict[str, list[str]]` mapping table name to its column node IDs, built once during graph construction and updated incrementally on refresh/delete. Column lookups become O(1) index lookup + O(columns_in_table) column iteration.
findings.impact: At 10,000 nodes, every catalog page load triggers a full graph scan. With frequent user navigation this is a noticeable latency source.

findings.title: _classify_transform: multi-pass AST walk (4 separate list(node.walk()) passes)
findings.description: The function calls `list(node.walk())` once at line 43 to get all_nodes, then iterates all_nodes four separate times (for Window, Cast, aggregation types, arithmetic types). The list() materialization itself traverses the tree once; the four for-loops add four more linear passes over the same materialized list. A single pass with early-exit logic would reduce the constant factor.
findings.evidence: sql.py:43: `all_nodes = list(node.walk())` then lines 45, 48, 51, 54: four separate `for n in all_nodes:` loops each checking different isinstance conditions.
findings.fix: Single-pass with ordered priority checks and break-on-first-match: `for n in node.walk(): if isinstance(n, exp.Window): return 'window', expr_str; if isinstance(n, exp.Cast): return 'cast', expr_str; ...`. Eliminates list materialization and 3 redundant passes.
findings.impact: Called once per SELECT column expression. At 50 columns per SELECT × 200 statements per file × 100 files = 1,000,000 calls. The constant factor improvement is real but each individual call is fast. P3.

residual_risks.description: nx.compose(state.lineage_graph, new_graph) at routes.py:225 copies the full combined graph into a new DiGraph on every refresh. With a large accumulated global graph, this is O(V+E) memory allocation plus NetworkX internal dict copies. Not reviewed here as unchanged code, but it interacts with PERF-02: the removal pass happens before compose, so the compose always operates on the minimal post-removal graph. Risk: if removal is slow (PERF-02), compose receives a stale graph for the window between removal and compose completion. Since this is synchronous and FastAPI runs in a single event loop, there is no concurrency hazard — but the latency window is additive.

_resolve_temp_views iterative chain resolution at sql.py:764-781: the outer loop runs `len(temp_views_lower) + 1` iterations; each iteration scans all entries in tv_sources (O(tv_entries)). For deeply chained temp view sequences in a single file (e.g., 20 chained views each with 50 columns = 1,000 tv_sources entries, 21 iterations), this is 21,000 iterations with string ops. Bounded by temp view count per file, not total graph size. Low risk at realistic scales but worth monitoring if view chain depths grow.

The DFS in _trace_raw_paths uses `steps_so_far + [step]` (line 402) to build path state, creating a new list on every recursion level. At max_depth=10 and branching factor B, this allocates O(B^depth) lists in the worst case before hitting the path cap. For dense graphs this could cause significant allocator pressure before truncated=True is set. Consider passing a mutable deque and appending/popping instead.

testing_gaps.description: No benchmark or regression test exists for _normalize_edges at scale. The O(N²) behavior in PERF-01 would only be caught by a test that measures wall time or operation count with N=500+ distinct table names. A pytest-benchmark fixture with a synthetic 500-table edge list would make this visible.

No test exercises the global-graph multi-source scenario (PERF-02): register 5 sources, refresh each, then verify that refresh of source 1 does not degrade as sources 2-5 grow the global graph. Without this, the O(total_edges) scan in refresh/delete is invisible in the test suite which uses single-source fixtures.

No test caps or measures filter/joinkey edge emission volume under complex WHERE clauses (PERF-05). A test with a 25-column WHERE clause across 100 statements would surface the edge inflation and make any future cap decision data-driven.

The _trace_raw_paths path cap (max_paths=50) has no test that verifies truncated=True is set and returned correctly for a graph with 100+ paths. Without this, a regression that removes the cap would not be caught.
